Algeria.text: Algeria scrape through from Group J in the most dramatic fashion possible: a 3-3 draw with Austria that had everything — a half-time equaliser from Belghali, Mahrez restoring parity midway through the second half, a 90th-minute Mahrez strike that looked to have sealed the point, and then Kalajdzic's 96th-minute reply that nearly wrenched it away entirely. They advance to face Switzerland in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT — a genuinely open contest between two sides of comparable pedigree, with Algeria's attacking threat balanced against their defensive fragility. Survive that and Colombia or Ghana await in the last sixteen, with the quarterfinal potentially producing a high-stakes North Africa derby against Egypt.

Argentina.text: Argentina navigated their final group assignment against Jordan with authority — two goals before half-time through Celso and a Martínez penalty, then Messi wrapping things up in the 80th despite a brief Jordan interruption midway through the second half. The defending champions top Group J without dropping a point. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a tie Argentina should control comfortably — the Islanders are a spirited lower-tier side, but the gulf in class makes this a routine assignment barring something extraordinary. Deeper in the draw, Colombia or Switzerland could test them at the quarterfinal stage, while a semifinal collision with Brazil, England, or Mexico would lift the tournament's stakes considerably.

Australia.text: A goalless draw with Paraguay to close out the group stage tells its own story: Australia edged through as runners-up on the back of cautious, functional football, with neither side willing to gamble and the prize of qualification enough. Egypt await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT — a winnable tie on paper, though the Egyptians are no soft touch and Australia's muted attacking output makes it a test of nerve as much as quality. Survive that, and the draw turns considerably darker: Argentina could stand in the way in the Round of 16, with the likes of Colombia or Brazil lurking further still.

Austria.text: Austria's group stage ended in the most breathless fashion imaginable: 3-3 against Algeria, with Kalajdzic's equaliser arriving deep into stoppage time to rescue a point after Mahrez had twice put the Algerians ahead — a rollercoaster that spoke more to defensive frailty than composure. That precarious second-place finish delivers a punishing reward in the Round of 32: Spain, on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a side of vastly greater pedigree and one most neutrals will expect to advance. Should Austria somehow find a way through, Croatia or Portugal would await in the Round of 16, with Belgium a possible quarter-final obstacle — steep climbs, every one of them.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed out Group G in emphatic fashion, dismantling New Zealand 5-1 in a performance that grew more commanding as the evening wore on — Trossard's brace set the tone, De Bruyne added a third, and late strikes from Lukaku and Saelemaekers left the margin flattering to no one. They meet Senegal in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT, a tie Belgium enter as clear favourites, though the Teranga Lions are no pushover and will demand genuine application. Survive that, and a potential quarterfinal collision with Portugal or Spain looms as the moment this campaign gets serious.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed the group stage with conviction, going two goals up inside 34 minutes against Qatar and, though Al-Haydos pulled one back before half-time, Mahmic's 80th-minute strike settled any nerves — a composed, professional performance when it mattered most. They meet the United States in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a genuinely daunting draw: the co-hosts carry crowd and expectation, and Bosnia, scraping through in third place, enter as clear underdogs who will need to reproduce that first-half sharpness to cause an upset. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, with Portugal or Spain lurking as possible quarterfinal opponents — the kind of names that would test far more fancied sides.

Brazil.text: Brazil edged past Japan in a nervy Round of 32 encounter, trailing to Sano's 29th-minute opener before Casemiro levelled midway through the second half and Martinelli spared blushes with a stoppage-time winner — hardly the statement a group winner expects. Norway await in the Round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, and while Brazil carry the weight of five-time champions' expectation, the Norwegians are no soft touch and will fancy disrupting a side that has yet to find its best rhythm. Survive that, and England or Argentina could loom as the tournament truly tightens.

Canada.text: Canada scraped past South Africa in nerve-shredding fashion, Eustáquio's stoppage-time winner sparing them a Round of 32 exit and delivering all three points with almost nothing to spare. Morocco await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — a genuinely stern examination, with the Atlas Lions' disciplined defensive structure and continental pedigree making this a real test rather than a formality for a Canadian side that has shown vulnerability. Survive that, and France could loom in the quarterfinal among others. Should Canada navigate that far, Portugal or Spain represent the kind of semifinal opponents that would demand an altogether different level.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde ground out a goalless draw with Saudi Arabia to close the group stage — a result that tells its own story: three draws from three, never beaten, never quite breaking through. Now the reward for that stubborn resilience is a Round of 32 appointment with Argentina on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture that, on any reasonable assessment, presents the Blue Sharks as considerable underdogs against one of the tournament's foremost contenders. Should they produce the upset of the competition, Australia or Egypt would await in the Round of 16, with Colombia or Algeria possible obstacles in the quarterfinal beyond that.

Colombia.text: Colombia top Group K in composed rather than spectacular fashion — a goalless draw with Portugal in the final round of group play confirms their solidity, a game in which neither side found a way through. They face Ghana in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a tie Colombia enter as clear favourites: the Black Stars are capable of an upset but lack the sustained quality to trouble a side that has conceded just once in the group. Should Colombia progress, Algeria or Switzerland await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina that would define the tournament's South American chapter entirely.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana in a group-stage finale that required a late intervention — Vlasic's 83rd-minute winner rescuing what had briefly become a precarious situation after Ghana levelled with seventeen minutes left. Now comes the sharper test: Portugal in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a genuinely demanding draw that asks Croatia to produce something beyond grit and organisation against one of the tournament's more complete sides. Should they find a way through, Spain or Austria await in the Round of 16, with Belgium or the United States possible obstacles in the quarterfinal beyond that.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo closed the group stage with real conviction, overturning Uzbekistan's early lead to win 3-1 — Wissa's penalty and a late brace either side of Mayele's strike making the final margin look comfortable after what had been a tighter contest for an hour. The reward is a Round of 32 date with England on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, and few neutrals will give the Leopards much chance there: England arrive as heavy favorites, and Congo will need to reproduce and exceed that second-half authority to cause a genuine upset. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico await in the last sixteen, with Brazil or Norway a possible quarterfinal — the kind of draw that flatters only the very brave.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador closed their group campaign with one of its genuine upsets — going behind to Sané's second-minute strike against Germany before Angulo levelled early and Plata's 77th-minute winner sealed a composed, hard-fought 2-1 victory that earned their passage as a best third-placed team. Mexico await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a fixture that carries the weight of a fierce continental rivalry; Ecuador arrive with belief restored, but the Mexicans, typically one of CONCACAF's most polished sides at major tournaments, make this a genuine coin-flip. Survive that, and either England or DR Congo could stand in the way in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil or Norway raises the stakes considerably.

Egypt.text: Egypt got through the group stage without losing, yet their finale against Iran carried the scent of unfinished business — Saber's fifth-minute opener surrendered inside nine minutes, the sides settling for a 1-1 draw that felt more like a negotiated peace than a statement. They go into the Round of 32 against Australia on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT as the team expected to advance, though the Socceroos are not without menace and Egypt's tendency to concede quickly will be tested. Survive that, and Argentina — the shadow looming over the Round of 16 — would represent an entirely different order of examination.

England.text: England closed out Group L with a composed if unspectacular 2-0 win over Panama, Bellingham and Kane striking within five minutes of each other midway through the second half to settle matters without drama. DR Congo await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a tie England will enter as firm favourites — the Leopards are dangerous on the counter but lack the pedigree to trouble a side of England's resources when functioning properly. Survive that, and Ecuador or Mexico could test them in the Round of 16 before a potential quarterfinal collision with Brazil or Norway raises the stakes considerably.

France.text: Ousmane Dembélé dismantled Norway almost single-handedly, completing a hat-trick inside 32 minutes before Doué added gloss deep in stoppage time — a 4-1 scoreline that flattered neither side, with France utterly dominant from the first whistle. They meet Sweden in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a fixture France enter as heavy favorites; the Swedes will make themselves hard to beat, but Les Bleus carry too much quality and momentum for this to feel like a genuine threat. Should they advance through Paraguay in the Round of 16, a quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT against Canada or Morocco looms as the first tie likely to test their composure — with Spain, Portugal, or Brazil potentially waiting in the latter stages if France are to go all the way.

Germany.text: Germany's tournament ends in familiar, painful fashion. They trailed to Enciso's first-half strike, levelled through Havertz after the break, and then surrendered to the shootout — a 3-4 defeat on penalties that felt grimly inevitable once extra time arrived. A group-stage finish of ten goals scored and six points suggested a side with real attacking momentum, yet Paraguay exposed the fragility lurking beneath it, and Germany are out in the Round of 32, bundled out by a team ranked far beneath them on reputation alone.

Ghana.text: Ghana scraped through the group stage as one of the best third-placed teams, but their final group outing against Croatia offered little comfort — Sucic opened the scoring before half-time, Luckassen pulled one back in the 73rd minute to raise hopes, then Vlasic killed the contest seven minutes from time in a defeat that felt deserved. Colombia await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, and the Cafeteros, with their tradition of technically gifted, physically imposing football, represent a stern assignment for a side that could not hold a lead. Survive that, and Algeria or Switzerland loom in the Round of 16, with Argentina potentially waiting in the quarterfinal should Ghana find improbable momentum.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text:
Wait — the brief shows `last_match` as a Round of 32 loss to Norway, and `eliminated` is false, yet there is no `road` array. This is contradictory: a team that lost in the Round of 32 would be eliminated and have no onward path.

Before I write the blurb, could you clarify? It looks like either:

- **`eliminated` should be `true`** (they lost to Norway and are out), or
- **the `road` array is missing** by accident and Ivory Coast actually progressed.

Which is correct? That determines whether I write a retrospective or a road-forward piece.

Japan.text: Japan held Brazil until the hour mark, Sano's 29th-minute opener giving them genuine belief before Casemiro levelled and Martinelli's stoppage-time winner completed a cruel late twist. That is how their tournament ends — not in a collapse but in heartbreak, undone in the final seconds of a contest they had controlled for long enough to dare to dream. Japan exit the 2026 World Cup with heads held high, their courage and organisation evident throughout a group stage that brought five points and seven goals, but the knockout rounds proved one step too far.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico completed the group stage in the manner of a team that has found its rhythm: a measured, professional dismantling of Czech Republic, with three second-half goals — the third a stoppage-time flourish — confirming a perfect nine points from three games and a clean sheet to finish. They enter the Round of 32 against Ecuador on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT as clear favorites, with the form and the pedigree to dispatch a South American side that will be competitive but not expected to prevail. Survive that, and England could await in the Round of 16 — a genuine examination — before the quarterfinal draw potentially delivers Brazil.

Morocco.text: Morocco survived the most dramatic of finishes against the Netherlands — trailing to CMemphis Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike, they conjured an equaliser through Diop in the first minute of stoppage time before holding their nerve to win 3-2 on penalties. That resilience sets up a Round of 16 meeting with Canada on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT, a tie Morocco will enter as clear favourites, the Atlas Lions' defensive solidity and big-game temperament giving them a considerable edge over a Canadian side still finding its footing on the world stage. Should they advance, France, Paraguay, or Sweden await in the quarterfinal on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT — France representing a genuinely stern examination of Morocco's continental ambitions. The semifinal bracket could yet deliver Spain, Portugal, or Belgium as further obstacles on what is shaping up as a serious tilt at the final.

Netherlands.text: There's a cruel symmetry to how the Netherlands exited: Gakpo's 72nd-minute strike looked to have settled a tense Round of 32 tie against Morocco, only for Diop to equalise in stoppage time and drag it to a shootout the Dutch lost 2-3. A tournament in which they topped Group F with seven points and a +10 goal difference ended not through any tactical failing but through the lottery of penalties, a brutal conclusion for a side that had looked one of the more convincing teams in the competition.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: Norway edged past Ivory Coast in a nervy Round of 32 tie that looked comfortable until Diallo pulled one back on 74 minutes, only for Haaland to settle matters with a late second four minutes from time. The reward is a Round of 16 appointment with Brazil on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT — a formidable draw by any measure, with the Seleção widely regarded as one of the tournament's heavyweights and Norway cast firmly as underdogs despite their last-gasp resilience. Survive that, and a quarterfinal path runs through England or Mexico among others, before a potential semifinal collision with Argentina or Colombia sharpens the imagination further.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: Paraguay dragged Germany into extra time and then penalties, Enciso's first-half strike cancelled out by Havertz just after the break, before the Albirroja held their nerve to win 4-3 in the shootout — a result that will be remembered as one of the tournament's great upsets. They now face either France or Sweden in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 14:00 PT; France would represent a formidable assignment for any side, though Paraguay arrive with belief freshly minted against one of the world's heavyweights. Should they progress, Canada or Morocco await in the quarterfinal, with Spain, Portugal, or the United States among those who could loom in the semifinal.

Portugal.text: Portugal ground out a goalless draw against Colombia in their final group fixture — a cautious, measured performance that secured passage from Group K in second place without ever threatening to ignite. They face Croatia in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a genuinely open contest between two sides with deep knockout pedigree; Portugal carry the weight of expectation, but Croatia have made a habit of inconveniencing favourites on the big stage. Win that, and Spain or Austria could await in the Round of 16, with France looming as a possible semifinal collision of genuine consequence.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal's group stage ended with a flourish rather than a whimper: a 5-0 demolition of Iraq, the goals arriving in a rush either side of the hour mark after Diarra's early opener, amounting to a statement of intent from a side that had otherwise stuttered through Group I. They advance as a best third-placed team, which means Belgium await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuine test, with the Belgians heavy favourites, though Senegal's attacking ferocity against Iraq will have earned a measure of respect. Survive that, and Portugal or Spain could loom in the quarterfinal.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain topped Group H without conceding a goal, and Baena's first-half strike against Uruguay — the only goal of a controlled, professional closing win — underlined a defensive solidity that has defined their tournament so far. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a tie Spain will enter as clear favourites; the Austrians are a tidy, well-organised side but lack the quality to trouble a team of this calibre across ninety minutes. Should Spain advance, the Round of 16 could bring either Croatia or Portugal — either proposition a genuine examination of their credentials, and the kind of European heavyweight clash that tends to define a tournament run.

Sweden.text: Sweden edged through a scrappy group-stage finale against Japan, Elanga's equaliser six minutes after Maeda's opener rescuing a point that proved just enough to carry them through as one of the best third-placed sides. The draw, tight and largely uninspiring, did little to suggest a team in commanding form. France await in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT — a formidable assignment by any measure, and Sweden enter it as clear underdogs against one of the tournament's heavyweight contenders. Survive that, and Paraguay stand in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Canada or Morocco.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland topped Group B with a composed second-half performance against Canada, Vargas and Manzambi striking either side of the hour to settle the contest before David's late consolation added only cosmetic tension. They meet Algeria in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie in which the Swiss enter as clear favourites — Algeria are a dangerous side on their day, but Switzerland's solidity and group-stage momentum make this a manageable assignment rather than a genuine threat. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana await in the Round of 16, with the daunting prospect of Argentina potentially looming in the quarterfinal.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

I don't want to invent a clean narrative around contradictory facts. Could you clarify:

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The USA closed out Group D with a painful defeat to Turkey — Güler's equaliser and Yilmaz's second goal putting the hosts on the back foot before Ayhan's stoppage-time winner made it 3-2, a gut-punch finish to what had been a competitive but ultimately ragged performance. It matters only so much: two earlier wins were enough to secure top spot. Bosnia & Herzegovina await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a fixture the USA enter as clear favourites against a side unlikely to trouble a team of this quality for long. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal loom in the Round of 16 — with the quarterfinal potentially delivering a far sterner examination in Portugal or Spain.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
